chapter2.translation: One hundred years of solitude (c.1) I many years later, in front of the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendía had to remember that remote afternoon when his father led him to know the ice.
Macondo was then a village of twenty mud and Cañabrava houses built at the edge of a river of diaphanous waters that rushed through a bed of polished stones, white and huge as prehistoric eggs.
The world was so recent, that many things lacked in its name, and to mention them you would have to point out with the finger.
Every year, for the month of March, a family of uprooted gypsies planted their tent near the village, and with a large fuss of pitos and timbales they announced the new inventions.
First they took the magnet.

A corpulent gypsy, from a mountain beard and gorrión hands, who appeared with the name of Melquiades, made a truculent public demonstration of what he himself called the eighth wonder of the wise alchemists of Macedonia.

He went home at home by dragging two metal bullions, and everyone was scared to see that the cauldrons, pailas, tongs and anaofes fell from their site, and the woods creak up the despair of the nails and screws trying to unleash, and even the lost objects for a long time appeared where they had more sought, and they had been sought for them, and they had been sought for them, and turbulent behind Melquiad's magical iron. "Things have their own life," the gypsy asked with a rough accent, "it's all a matter of waking the soul." José Arcadio Buendía, whose unfortunate imagination was always further than the ingenuity of nature, and even beyond miracle and magic, thought it was possible to use that useless invention to unravel the gold of the earth.

Melquiades, who was an honest man, prevented him: "For that it doesn't work." But José Arcadio Buendía did not believe at that time in the honesty of the gypsies, so he changed his mule and a game of goats for the two imantados linges.
Úrsula Iguarán, his wife, who had those animals to widen the excessive domestic heritage, failed to deter it. "Gold to cobble the house is very soon," replied her husband.
For several months he insisted on demonstrating the success of his conjectures.
He explored the region, including the background of the river, dragging the two iron bullion and reciting the Melquiad spell aloud.
The only thing that managed to dig up was an armor of the fifteenth century with all its parts welded by an oxide helmet, whose interior had hollow resonance of a huge pumpkin full of stones.

When José Arcadio Buendía and the four men of his expedition managed to dismantle the armor, they found inside a calcified skeleton that had been hung on the neck a copper reliquary with a woman's curl.
In March the gypsies returned.
This time they carried a catalejo and a magnifying glass of the size of a drum, which they exhibited as the last discovery of Amsterdam's Jews.
They sat a gypsy at one end of the village and installed the catalejo at the entrance of the tent.

Through the payment of five reais, people looked out the Catalejo and saw the gypsy at your fingertips. "Science has eliminated distances," Melquiades proclaimed. "Soon, man can see what happens anywhere on earth, without moving from his house." A burning noon made an amazing demonstration with the gigantic magnifying glass: they put a lot of dry grass in the middle of the street and set fire to him by concentration of the sun's rays.
José Arcadio Buendía, who still had no consolidated because of the failure of his magnets, conceived the idea of ​​using that invention as a gun of war.
Melquiades, again, tried to deter it.
But he ended up accepting the two magnetized bears and three pieces of colonial money in exchange for the magnifying glass.
Úrsula cried with consternation.
That money was part of a gold coin chest that her father had accumulated in a lifetime of deprivation, and that she had buried under the bed waiting for a good occasion to invest.
José Arcadio Buendía did not even try to comfort her, delivered entirely to her tactical experiments with the self -denial of a scientist and even at the risk of her own life.

Trying to demonstrate the effects of the magnifying glass on the enemy troop, he exposed himself to the concentration of the sun's rays and suffered burns that became ulcers and took a long time to heal.
Before his wife's protests, alarmed by such dangerous inventiveness, he was about to set the house on fire.
He spent long hours in his room, making calculations on the strategic possibilities of his novel weapon, until he managed to compose an amazing didactic clarity and an irresistible conviction power.

He sent it to the authorities accompanied by numerous testimonies about their experiences and several explanatory drawings, in the care of a messenger that crossed the mountains, and was lost in excessive swamps, traced rivers torments and was about to perish under the scourge of beasts, despair and plague, before getting a link route with the email mules.

Although the trip to the capital was at that time little less than impossible, José Arcadio Buendia promised to try as soon as the government was ordered, in order to make practical demonstrations of his invention before the military powers, and train them personally in the complicated arts of the solar war.
For several years he waited for the answer.

Finally, tired of waiting, he lamented before Melquiades of the failure of his initiative, and the gypsy then gave a convincing evidence of honesty: he returned the beachs in exchange for the magnifying glass, and also left him some Portuguese maps and several navigation instruments.
From his handwriting he wrote a tight synthesis of the studies of the monk Hermann, which he left at his disposal so that he could use the astrolabio, the compass and the sextant.
José Arcadio Buendía spent the long months of rain locked in a little room that he built at the bottom of the house so that no one disturbed his experiments.

Having completely abandoned domestic obligations, he remained entire nights in the courtyard watching the course of the stars, and was about to contract an insolation for trying to establish an exact method to find noon.

When he became an expert in the use and handling of his instruments, he had a notion of space that allowed him to navigate in unknown seas, visit uninhabited territories and lock relationship with splendid beings, without abandoning his cabinet.

That was the time when he acquired the habit of speaking alone, walking around the house without paying attention to anyone, while Úrsula and the children split the spine in the garden taking care of the banana and the malanga, the cassava and the yam, the ahuyama and the eggplant.
Suddenly, without any ad, its feverish activity was interrupted and replaced by a kind of fascination.
He spent several days as a bewitched, repeating himself in a low voice a sartal of amazing conjectures, without giving credit to his own understanding.
Finally, on a Tuesday in December, at lunchtime, he released the entire load of his torment.

The children had to remember for the rest of their life the august solemnity with which their father sat at the head of the table, trembling with fever, devastated by the prolonged vigil and by the gate of his imagination, and revealed their discovery. "The earth is round as an orange."
Úrsula lost patience. "If you have to go crazy, come with yourself," he shouted.

But don't try to instill your gypsy ideas to children. " José Arcadio Buendía, impassive, did not allow himself to be intimidated by the despair of his wife, who in an abduction of cholera destroyed the astrolabio against the ground.

He built another, gathered in the little room the men of the people and showed them, with theories that were incomprehensible for everyone, the possibility of returning to the starting point always sailing to the East.
The entire village was convinced that José Arcadio Buendía had lost his mind, when Melquiades arrived to put things at his point.

He exalted in public the intelligence of that man who by pure astronomical speculation had built a theory already proven in practice, although unknown until Macondo, and as a proof of his admiration he gave him a gift that was to exert a strict influence in the future of the village: an alchemy laboratory.
At that time, Melquiades had aged with amazing speed.
On his first trips he seemed to be the same age of José Arcadio Buendia.
But while he retained his huge strength, which allowed him to tear down a horse grabbing him by his ears, the gypsy seemed stranded by a tenacious ailment.
It was, in reality, the result of multiple and rare diseases contracted in its countless trips around the world.
According to himself he told José Arcadio Buendia as he helped him mount the laboratory, death followed him everywhere, sniffing his pants, but without deciding to give him the final bundle.
It was a fugitive of how many pests and catastrophes had scourge the human race.

He survived La Pelagra in Persia, scurvy in the Malaysia archipelago, leprosy in Alexandria, Beriberi in Japan, bubonic plague in Madagascar, to the earthquake of Sicily and a massive wreck in the Magellan Strait.
That prodigious being who said he had Nostradamus's keys, was a dreary man, wrapped in a sad aura, with an Asian look that seemed to know the other side of things.
He wore a large and black hat, like the wings extended to a crow, and a velvet vest skated by the greenish of the centuries.
But despite his immense wisdom and his mysterious scope, he had a human weight, a land condition that kept him entangled in the tiny problems of everyday life.
He complained about old ailments, suffered from the most insignificant economic mishaps and had stopped laughing for a long time, because the scurvy had torn off his teeth.
The suffocating noon in which he revealed his secrets, José Arcadio Buendía had the certainty that this was the beginning of a great friendship.
The children were amazed with their fantastic stories.

Aureliano, who was not then more than five years old, had to remember him for the rest of his life as he saw that afternoon, sitting against the metallic and reverberant clarity of the window, lighting with his pro-founded voice of organ the darkest territories of the imagination, while dripping through his temples the fat melted by heat.
José Arcadio, his older brother, had to convey that wonderful image, as a hereditary memory, to all his offspring.

Úrsula, on the other hand, retained a bad memory of that visit, because he entered the room at the time Melquiads broke a bottle of mercury biclloride by distraction. "It's the smell of the demon," she said. "In absolute," Melquiades defended.
It is proven that the demon has sulphuric properties, and this is nothing more than a little Soliman.
Always didactic, he made a wise exposure on the diabolical virtues of the Cinabrio, but Úrsula ignored him, but took the children to pray.
That mordant smell would be forever in his memory, linked to the memory of Melquiades.

The rudimentary laboratory - without counting a profusion of casseroles, funnels, returns, filters and strainers - was composed of a primitive ties; A long and narrow neck crystal specimen, imitation of the philosophical egg, and a distiller built by the gypsies themselves according to the modern descriptions of the three arms of Mary the Jew.

In addition to these things, Melquiades left signs of the seven metals corresponding to the seven planets, the formulas of Moses and Zósimo for the bending of gold, and a series of notes and drawings on the processes of the great teaching, which allowed those who knew how to interpret them try the manufacture of the philosopher's stone.

Seduced by the simplicity of the formulas to bend the gold, José Arcadio Buendía courted Úrsula for several weeks, to allow him to dig up his colonial currencies and increase them as many times as possible to subdivide the azogile.
Úrsula gave, as always happened, before the unwavering of her husband.
Then José Arcadio Buendía thirty folds in a casserole, and melted them with copper, oropiment, sulfur and lead scrape.
He boiled everything over a living fire in a castle of castor oil until he obtained a thick and pestilent syrup more similar to vulgar candy than to magnificent gold.

In random and desperate distillation processes, melted with the seven planetary metals, worked with the hermetic mercury and the chipre vitriol, and return to cook in lard in the absence of radish oil, the precious heritage of Úrsula was reduced to a carbonized pork rind that could not be detached from the bottom of the cauldero.
When the gypsies returned, Úrsula had predisposed against them to the entire population.

But curiosity could more than fear, because that time the gypsies toured the village making a deafening noise with all kinds of musician instruments, while the preacher announced the exhibition of the most fabulous finding of the Nascians.
So everyone went to the tent, and by paying a penny they saw a youthful melquiads, spare, unbridled, with a new and radiant denture.
Those who remembered their gums destroyed by the scurvy, their flaccid cheeks and their withered lips, shuddered to fear before that strict proof of the supernatural powers of the gypsy.

The dread became panic when Melquiades took out his teeth, intact, set in the gums, and showed them to the public for a moment a fleeting moment in which he was again the same decrepit man of the previous years and put them again and smiled again with a full domain of his restored youth.

Even José Arcadio Buendía himself considered that Melquiad's knowledge had reached intolerable extremes, but experienced a healthy joy when the gypsy explained to Solas the mechanism of his denture postizes.

That seemed at the same time so simple and prodigious, that overnight he lost all interest in alchemy investigations; He suffered a new crisis in a bad mood, did not eat again regularly and spent the day spinning around the house. "Incredible things are happening in the world," he told Úrsula.

There, on the other side of the river, there are all kinds of magical devices, while we continue to live like donkeys. » Those who knew him since the time of the foundation of Macondo, were amazed at how much he had changed under the influence of Melquiades.

At first, José Arcadio Buendía was a kind of youth patriarch, which gave instructions for planting and advice for raising children and animals, and collaborated with everyone, even in physical work, for the good march of the community.
Since his house was from the first moment the best in the village, the others were fixed in his image and likeness.

He had a wide and well -lit salita, a terrace -shaped dining room with joyful flowers, two bedrooms, a patio with a gigantic chestnut, a well -planted garden and a corral where the goats, pigs and chickens lived in the Pacific community.
The only animals prohibited not only in the house, but throughout the town, were the fighting roosters.
The industriousness of Úrsula was walking with that of her husband.

Active, small, severe, that woman of unwavering nerves, whom at no time in her life she heard her sing, seemed to be everywhere from dawn to very entrance the night, always persecuted by the soft whisper of her dicks of Olán.

Thanks to her, the beaten land floors, the mud walls without praying, the rustic wooden furniture built by themselves were always clean, and the old arcons where the clothes were stored exhaled a lukewarm.

José Arcadio Buendía, who was the most entrepreneurial man who would ever be seen in the village, had in such a way the position of the houses, which from all could reach the river and stock up with water with equal effort, and drew the streets with such good sense that no house received more sun than another at the time of heat.
In a few years, Macondo was a more orderly and laborious village than any of those known until then for its 300 inhabitants.
It was really a happy village, where no one was over thirty years old and where no one had died.
Since the time of the Foundation, José Arcadio Buendía built traps and cages.
In a short time he filled with turpials, canaries, tiles and petirrojos not only the house itself, but all of the village.
The concert of so many different birds became so stunned, that Úrsula covered his ears with beeswax so as not to lose the sense of reality.

The first time the tribe of Melquiades arrived selling glass balls for the headache, everyone was surprised that they could have found that village lost in the siph of the swamp, and the gypsies confessed that they had been oriented by the song of the birds.
That spirit of social initiative disappeared in a short time, dragged by the fever of magnets, astronomical calculations, the dreams of transmutation and the desire to know the wonders of the world.
As an entrepreneur and clean, José Arcadio Buendía became a man with a lazy appearance, careless in dress, with a wild beard that Úrsula managed to quote hard with a kitchen knife.
There was no need to consider him a victim of some strange spell.

But even the most convinced of his madness left work and families to follow him, when his tools to disassemble, and asked for everyone's contest to open a trail that put Macondo in contact with the great inventions.
José Arcadio Buendía completely ignored the geography of the region.

He knew that towards the East was the Impenetrable Sierra, and on the other side of the Sierra the ancient city of Riohacha, where in past times - according to the first Aureliano Buendía, his grandfather - Sir Francis Drake was given to the sport of hunting caimanes to guns, which then made to pray and fill in straw to take them to Queen Isabel.

In his youth, he and his men, with women and children and animals and all kinds of household goods, crossed the mountains looking for an exit to the sea, and after twenty -six months they gave up the company and founded Macondo so as not to have to take the way back.
It was, therefore, a route that did not interest him, because he could only lead him to the past.
To the south were the swamps, covered with an eternal vegetable cream, and the vast universe of Ciénaga Grande, which according to the testimony of the gypsies lacked limits.
The Ciénaga Grande confused the West with an aquatic extension without horizons, where there were delicate skin with a woman's head and torso, who lost navigators with the spell of their huge tits.
The gypsies sailed six months on that route before reaching the mainland belt where the email mules passed.
According to the calculations of José Arcadio Buendía, the only possibility of contact with civilization was the Northern Route.

So he endowed with disassembly tools and hunting weapons to the same men who accompanied him in the foundation of Macondo; He threw his guidance instruments and his maps in a backpack, and undertook the reckless adventure.
The first days did not find an appreciable obstacle.
They descended by the stony Ribera del Río to the place where years before they had found the warrior armor, and there they penetrated the forest by a path of wild orange.
At the end of the first week, they killed and roasted a deer, but they were satisfied with eating half and saving the rest for the next few days.
They tried to postpone with that precaution the need to continue eating macaws, whose blue meat had a rough musk flavor.
Then, for more than ten days, the sun did not see again.

The ground became soft and wet, as a volcanic ash, and the vegetation was increasingly insidious and the screams of the birds and the bullaranga of the monkeys became further, and the world became sad forever.

The men of the expedition felt overwhelmed by their oldest memories in that paradise of moisture and silence, prior to the original sin, where the boots sank into smoking wells and the machetes shattered bloody lilies and golden salamanders.

For a week, almost without speaking, they advanced as sleepwalkers by a universe of sorrow, barely lighting by a dim reverberation of light insects and with the lungs overwhelmed by a suffocating smell of blood.

They could not return, because the trail they were opening in their path was closed again in a short time, with a new vegetation that almost saw grow before their eyes. "No matter," said José Arcadio Buendía.
The essential thing is not to lose orientation. " Always pending compass, he continued to guide his men to the invisible north, until they managed to leave the enchanted region.
It was a dense night, without stars, but the darkness was impregnated by a new and clean air.
Exhausted by the prolonged journey, they hung the hammocks and thoroughly slept for the first time in two weeks.
When they woke up, already with the high sun, they were stunned of fascination.
In front of them, surrounded by ferns and palm trees, white and dusty in the silent morning light, there was a huge Spanish galleon.
Slightly turned to the starboard, of its intact tree hung the squalid piltrafas of the Velamn, including orchids adorned.
The helmet, covered with a smooth shell of Petrified Rémora and tender moss, was firmly nestled on a stones floor.
The entire structure seemed to occupy its own scope, a space of loneliness and oblivion, forbidden the vices of time and customs of the birds.
Inside, that the expeditionaries explored with a stealthy fervor, there was nothing more than a tight flower forest.
The finding of the Galeon, a indication of the proximity of the sea, broke the impetus of José Arcadio Buendía.

He considered as a mockery of his mischievous destiny to have sought the sea without finding it, at the price of sacrifices and penalties without a story, and then found it without looking for him, crossed in his way as an insurmountable obstacle.

Many years later, Colonel Aureliano Buendía returned to the region, when it was already a regular route of the mail, and the only thing he found of the ship was the carbonized rib in the middle of a poppy field.
Only then convinced that this story had not been an engendro of his father's imagination, he wondered how the galleon had to enter that point on the mainland.
But José Arcadio Buendía did not consider that concern when he found the sea, after another four days of travel, twelve kilometers away from the galleon.
His dreams ended in front of that ash, sparkling and dirty sea, which did not deserve the risks and sacrifices of his adventure. "Show!" -shout-.
Macondo is surrounded by water everywhere.
The idea of ​​a peninsular Macondo prevailed for a long time, inspired by the arbitrary map that José Arcadio Buendía drew when he returned from his expedition.

He drew it with rage, exhausting in bad faith the difficulties of communication, as well as to punish himself for the absolute lack of meaning with which he chose the place. "We will never get anywhere," he said before Úrsula.

Here we have to rot in life without receiving the benefits of science. » That certainty, rumped for several months in the laboratory room, led him to conceive the project of transferring Macondo to a more conducive place.
But this time, Úrsula anticipated his febrile designs.
In a secret and implacable aptic work, he predisposed to the women of the village against the veleity of their men, who were already beginning to prepare for the move.
José Arcadio Buendía did not know at what time, nor by virtue of what adverse forces, his plans were entangled in a tangle of pretexts, setbacks and evasive, until becoming pure and simple illusion.

Úrsula observed him with innocent attention, and even felt a little mercy for him, the morning he found him in the background of the background commenting on his dream dreams, while placing the pieces of the laboratory in his original boxes.
He let him finish.

He let him nail the boxes and put his initials on top with an inked swab, without having any reproach, but knowing that he knew (because he heard it to say in his deaf monologues) that the men of the town would not support him in his company.

Only when he began to disassemble the little room, Ursula dared to ask him why he did it, and he replied with a certain bitterness: "Since nobody wants to leave, we will leave alone." Úrsula did not alter. "We'll leave," he said.
Here we stay, because here we have had a child. "We still have no dead," he said.
One is nowhere as long as he does not have a dead person under the earth.
Úrsula replied, with a gentle firmness: "If you need to die to stay here, I die."
José Arcadio Buendía did not believe that his wife's will was so rigid.

He tried to seduce her with the spell of her fantasy, with the promise of a prodigious world where she was enough to throw magical fluids on earth so that the plants did fruits at the will of man, and where all kinds of pain devices were sold at a trinued price.
But Úrsula was insensitive to its clairvoyance. "At the same time, we think about your crazy noveleria, you must take care of your children," he replied.
Look at them how they are, abandoned to God's good, just like donkeys.
José Arcadio Buendía took his wife's words to the letter.
He looked through the window and saw the two barefoot children in the sunny garden, and had the impression that only at that moment they had begun to exist, conceived by the Ursula spell.
Something happened then inside; Something mysterious and definitive that uprooted him from his current time and led him to drift by an unexplored region of the remes.

While Ursula continued to sweep the house that was now sure not to leave in the rest of his life he remained contemplating the children with an absorbed look until the eyes wet and dried them with the back of his hand, and exhaled a deep sigh of resignation. "Well," he said.
Tell them to come to help me get things out of the drawers.
José Arcadio, the oldest of the children, had turned fourteen.
He had a square head, hirsute hair and the voluntary character of his father.
Although he had the same impulse of growth and physical strength, since then it was evident that he lacked imagination.
He was conceived and given birth during the painful journey of the Sierra, before the foundation of Macondo, and his parents thanks to heaven when he did not have any animal organ.
Aureliano, the first human being that was born in Macondo, was going to turn six in March.
It was silent and withdrawn.
He had cried in his mother's belly and was born with his eyes open.
While cutting the navel moved his head from side to side recognizing the things of the room, and examined people's face with a curiosity without astonishment.
Then, indifferent to those who approached to meet him, he kept concentrated attention on the palm roof, which seemed about to collapse under the tremendous rainfall.

Ursula did not remember the intensity of that look until a day when little Aureliano, at the age of three, entered the kitchen at the time she withdrew from the stove and put on the table a pot of boiling broth.

The child, perplexed at the door, said: "He is going to fall." The pot was well placed in the center of the table, but as soon as the child made the announcement, began an irrevocable movement towards the edge, as driven by an inner dynamism, and torn apart on the ground.
Úrsula, alarmed, told her husband's episode, but she interpreted him as a natural phenomenon.

Thus it was always, oblivious to the existence of his children, partly because he considered childhood as a period of mental insufficiency, and partly because he was always too absorbed in his own chimeric speculations.
But from the afternoon he called the children to help him unpack the things of the laboratory, he dedicated their best hours.

In the fourteenth, whose walls were gradually filled with unlikely maps and fabulous graphics, taught them to read and write and get accounts, and told them about the wonders of the world not only as far as their knowledge reached him, but forcing the limits of his imagination to incredible extremes.

This is how the children ended up learning that at the southern end of Africa there were men so intelligent and peaceful that their only entertainment was to sit down to think, and that it was possible to cross the Aegean Sea jumping from Island to Island to the port of Salares.

Those amazing sessions were in such a way printed in the memory of the children, that many years later, a second before the regular armies officer gave the fire order to the firing squad, Colonel Aurelian and drums and rattles of the gypsies who once again reached the village, proclaiming the last and amazing discovery of Memphis's sages.
They were new gypsies.

Young men and women who only knew their own tongue, beautiful copies of oil and intelligent hands, whose dances and music sowed in the streets a panic of ruffled joy, with their painted parrots of all the colors that recited Italian romances, and the chicken that put a hundred golden eggs to the sound of the tambour To glue buttons and lower the fever, and the device to forget the bad memories, and the plaster to waste time, and a thousand more inventions, so ingenious and unusual, that José Arcadio Buendía would have wanted to invent the memory machine to be able to remember all.
In an instant they transformed the village.
The inhabitants of Macondo were suddenly lost in their own streets, stunned by the mass fair.

Carrying a child of each hand so as not to lose them in the tumult, stumbling with goldsmith's goldsbase of gold and jugglers of six arms, stifled by the confusing encouragement of manure and sandalwood that exhaled the crowd, José Arcadio Buendía was like a madman looking for Melquiades everywhere, to reveal the infinite secrets of that infinite secret.
He addressed several gypsies who did not understand their language.
Finally, he reached the place where Melquiads used to plant his store, and found a Taciturn Armenian who announced in Spanish a syrup to become invisible.
A cup of the Ambarine substance had been taken from a blow, when José Arcadio Buendía made way for pushing between the absorbed group that witnessed the show, and managed to ask the question.

The gypsy wrapped him in the stunned climate of his gaze, before becoming a puddle of pestilent and smoking tar on which the resonance of his response was floating: "Melquiades died." Stunned by the news, José Arcadio Buendía remained motionless, trying to overcome the affliction, until the group dispersed claimed by other artifice and the puddle of the Taciturn Armenian was completely evaporated.
Later, other gypsies confirmed that in effect Melquiads had succumbed to fevers in the Médans of Singapore, and his body had been thrown into the deepest place of the Java Sea.
Children were not interested in the news.
They were stubborn that their father took them to know the portentous novelty of Memphis's sages, announced at the entrance of a store that, they said, belonged to King Solomon.

They insisted so much that José Arcadio Buendía paid the thirty reais and led them to the center of the tent, where there was a hairy torso giant and shaved head, with a copper ring in the nose and a heavy iron chain in the ankle, guarding a pirate chest.
Being uncovered by the giant, the chest let a glacial breath escape.
Inside there was only a huge transparent block, with infinite internal needles in which the clarity of the twilight was torn apart in colored stars.
Beclected, knowing that the children expected an immediate explanation, José Arcadio Buendía dared to murmur: "It's the largest diamond in the world." "No," the gypsy corred.
José Arcadio Buendía, without understanding, extended his hand towards the weather, but the giant separated it. "Five more reais to touch it," he said.
José Arcadio Buendía paid them, and then put his hand on the ice, and kept it on for several minutes, while the heart swells of fear and joy at the contact of the mystery.
Without knowing what to say, he paid another ten reais for his children to live the prodigious experience.
Little José Arcadio refused to touch him.
Aureliano, on the other hand, took a step forward, put his hand and withdrew it on the spot. "He is boiling," he exclaimed scared.
But his father did not pay attention to him.
Evidence of prodigy, at that time he forgot about the frustration of his delusional companies and the body of Melquiades abandoned to the appetite of the squid.
He paid five other reais, and with his hand on the weathering, as expressing a testimony about the sacred text, he exclaimed: "This is the great invention of our time."

chapter3.translation: One hundred years of Soledad (C.1) II When the Pirate Francis Drake assaulted Riohacha, in the 16th century, the great -grandmother of Úrsula Iguarán was scared so much with the touch of rebat and the patterning of the cannons, which lost control of the nerves and sat on a fired stove.
The burns left her turned into a useless wife for a lifetime.
He could not sit in the middle side, accommodated in cushions, and something strange had to stay in the way of walking, because he never walked again in public.
He resigned from all kinds of social habits obsessed with the idea that his body dismissed a smell of scorch.

Alba surprised her in the patio without daring to sleep, because she dreamed that the English with their fierce assault dogs got through the bedroom window and subjected her to shameful torments with red irons alive.
Her husband, an Aragonese merchant with whom she had two children, spent half a store on medicines and entertainments looking for ways to relieve her terrors.

Finally he liquidated the business and led the family to live away from the sea, in a ranchería of Pacific Indians located in the foothills of the Sierra, where he built his wife a bedroom without windows so they did not have where the pirates of their nightmares enter.

In the hidden ranchería lived a long time ago a Creole tobacco cultivator, Don José Arcadio Buendía, with whom the great -grandfather of Úrsula established such a productive society that in a few years they made a fortune.
Several centuries later, the Creole great -grandson married the great -grandson of the Aragonese.
